message.content: Adversaries may exploit Microsoft Office templates to gain persistence on compromised systems by embedding malicious macros in base templates. These templates, such as Word's Normal.dotm and Excel's Personal.xlsb, are loaded each time the respective Office application starts. Attackers can modify these templates to execute code automatically, thereby maintaining persistence. They may also hijack the application's search order or modify registry keys like GlobalDotName to point to malicious templates. Common exploitation methods include using VBA macros to execute code, with tools like BackConfig and Cobalt Strike leveraging hidden columns or trusted macro settings to bypass user permissions. Mitigation strategies include disabling macros, restricting macro execution to trusted locations, monitoring and controlling template file paths, and enforcing strict security policies on macro usage. Regularly auditing and monitoring registry keys and template locations can also help detect and prevent such abuses.
